chunk_summary: Guidelines for outpatient feeding of non-breastfed children aged 6–24 months, focusing on meal frequency, energy density, milk intake, food safety, and monitoring hydration.
learning_objectives: Understand the feeding requirements for non-breastfed children aged 6–24 months.
Learn how to set meal frequency and milk volume targets for optimal nutrition.
Identify strategies to ensure food safety and hygiene in resource-constrained settings.
Recognize the importance of monitoring hydration and iron intake in non-breastfed children.
key_facts: Non-breastfed children require 4–5 meals per day with 1–2 nutritious snacks if appetite allows.
Milk intake should range from ~200–400 mL/day with regular animal-source foods or ~300–500 mL/day if milk is the primary protein source.
Avoid prolonged milk sipping or bottle use to prevent appetite suppression for solids.
Strict hygiene practices include handwashing, clean utensils, and serving food immediately after preparation.
Cooked foods should follow the '2-hour rule' at room temperature and be reheated thoroughly.
reference_quote: Non-breastfed infants and toddlers are at higher risk because the total energy requirement that must come from foods is higher and infection risk increases when safe water and hygiene are constrained. (WHO Guideline for complementary feeding of infants and young children 6–23 months of age)
document_key_topics: Meal frequency and energy density targets for non-breastfed children
Milk intake guidelines and iron protection strategies
Food safety and hygiene practices in outpatient feeding
